story: According to a 2011 Pew Research Center survey, there were 2.2 billion Christians around the world in 2010, up from about 600 million in 1910. By 2050, the Christian population is expected to exceed 3 billion. According to a 2012 Pew Research Center survey Christianity will remain the world's largest religion in 2050, if current trends continue.
q1: In 2010, Christianity had fewer than 2 billion adherents. (true/false)
q2: Christianity is projected to decline significantly by 2050. (true/false)

story: The earliest occurrences of the term in non-Christian literature include Josephus, referring to "the tribe of Christians, so named from him;" Pliny the Younger in correspondence with Trajan; and Tacitus, writing near the end of the 1st century. In the Annals he relates that "by vulgar appellation [they were] commonly called Christians" and identifies Christians as Nero's scapegoats for the Great Fire of Rome.
q1: The Annals was written by Josephus. (true/false)
q2: Tacitus blamed Christians for the Great Fire of Rome. (true/false)

story: As of the early 21st century, Christianity has approximately 2.4 billion adherents. The faith represents about a third of the world's population and is the largest religion in the world. Christians have composed about 33 percent of the world's population for around 100 years. The largest Christian denomination is the Roman Catholic Church, with 1.17 billion adherents, representing half of all Christians.
q1: The Roman Catholic Church is a minority denomination within Christianity. (true/false)
q2: Christianity became the world's largest religion only in the past 20 years. (true/false)

story: According to Scientific Elite: Nobel Laureates in the United State by Harriet Zuckerman, a review of American Nobel prizes awarded between 1901 and 1972, 72% of American Nobel Prize laureates identified a Protestant background. Overall, Protestants have won a total of 84.2% of all the Nobel Prizes in Chemistry, 60% in Medicine, and 58.6% in Physics awarded to Americans between 1901 and 1972.
q1: Catholics won more Nobel Prizes in Chemistry than Protestants between 1901 and 1972. (true/false)
q2: 72% of American Nobel laureates had Protestant backgrounds. (true/false)

story: Christians have made a myriad contributions in a broad and diverse range of fields, including the sciences, arts, politics, literatures and business. According to 100 Years of Nobel Prizes, a review of Nobel prizes awarded between 1901 and 2000 reveals that (65.4%) of Nobel Prizes laureates identified Christianity in its various forms as their religious preference.
q1: 65.4% of Nobel Prize laureates between 1901 and 2000 identified as Christian. (true/false)
q2: Christians have worked only in politics according to the passage. (true/false)

story: Christians have made a myriad contributions in a broad and diverse range of fields, including the sciences, arts, politics, literatures and business. According to 100 Years of Nobel Prizes, a review of Nobel prizes awarded between 1901 and 2000 reveals that (65.4%) of Nobel Prizes laureates identified Christianity in its various forms as their religious preference.
q1: The Nobel Prizes review covered the years 1920 to 2020. (true/false)
q2: Christians have contributed to sciences and arts according to this passage. (true/false)

story: In 2004, SME and Bertelsmann Music Group merged as Sony BMG Music Entertainment. When Sony acquired BMG's half of the conglomerate in 2008, Sony BMG reverted to the SME name. The buyout led to the dissolution of BMG, which then relaunched as BMG Rights Management. Out of the "Big Three" record companies, with Universal Music Group being the largest and Warner Music Group, SME is middle-sized.
q1: SME merged with Bertelsmann in 2004. (true/false)
q2: SME is the largest record company of the Big Three. (true/false)

story: In 1929, ARC was founded through a merger of several smaller record companies, which, ultimately, transformed into one enterprise known as SME. In the depths of the Great Depression, the Columbia Phonograph Company (founded in 1888) in the U.S. (including its Okeh Records subsidiary) was acquired by ARC in 1934.
q1: Columbia Phonograph Company was founded in the 1900s. (true/false)
q2: ARC acquired Columbia Phonograph Company in 1934. (true/false)

story: Columbia founded Epic Records in 1953. In 1956, Conkling left Columbia, he would help establish the National Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences before eventually becoming the first president of the newly launched Warner Bros. Records, and Goddard Lieberson began the first of two stints as head of the record company. In 1958, Columbia founded another label, Date Records, which initially issued rockabilly music.
q1: Epic Records was founded by Columbia in 1953. (true/false)
q2: Conkling was the first president of Epic Records. (true/false)

story: Also in late 1965, the Date subsidiary label was revived. This label released the first string of hits for Peaches & Herb and scored a few minor hits from various other artists. Date's biggest success was "Time of the Season" by the Zombies, peaking at #2 in 1969. The label was discontinued in 1970.
q1: The Date label was founded in 1960. (true/false)
q2: The Date label was discontinued in 1970. (true/false)

story: In March 1968, CBS and Sony formed CBS/Sony Records, a Japanese business joint venture. With Sony being one of the developers behind the compact disc digital music media, a compact disc production plant was constructed in Japan under the joint venture, allowing CBS to begin supplying some of the first compact disc releases for the American market in 1983.
q1: CBS/Sony Records was established in March 1968. (true/false)
q2: Sony did not contribute to compact disc development. (true/false)

story: The CBS Records Group was led very successfully by Clive Davis until his dismissal in 1972, after it was discovered that Davis has used CBS funds to finance his personal life, including an expensive bar mitzvah party for his son. He was replaced first by former head Goddard Lieberson, then in 1975 by the colourful and controversial lawyer Walter Yetnikoff, who led the company until 1990.
q1: Clive Davis was dismissed from CBS Records in 1972. (true/false)
q2: Walter Yetnikoff became CEO of CBS Records in 1975. (true/false)

story: In February 2016, over a hundred thousand people signed a petition in just twenty-four hours, calling for a boycott of Sony Music and all other Sony-affiliated businesses after rape allegations against music producer Dr. Luke were made by musical artist Kesha. Kesha asked a New York City Supreme Court to free her from her contract with Sony Music but the court denied the request, prompting widespread public and media response.
q1: Over 100,000 people signed a petition for boycott in 2016. (true/false)
q2: The court granted Kesha's request to leave her contract. (true/false)

story: By 1987, CBS was the only "big three" American TV network to have a co-owned record company. ABC had sold its record division to MCA Records in 1979, and in 1986, NBC's parent company RCA was sold to General Electric, who then sold off all other RCA units, including the record division (which was bought by Ariola Records, later known as BMG).
q1: ABC sold its record division in 1979. (true/false)
q2: By 1987 all three big networks owned record companies. (true/false)

story: On November 17, 1987, SCA acquired CBS Records, which hosted such acts as Michael Jackson, for US$2 billion. CBS Inc., now CBS Corporation, retained the rights to the CBS name for music recordings but granted Sony a temporary license to use the CBS name. CBS Corporation founded a new CBS Records in 2006, which is distributed by Sony through its RED subsidiary.
q1: SCA acquired CBS Records for US$2 billion in 1987. (true/false)
q2: Michael Jackson was the sole artist on CBS Records. (true/false)

story: RCA/Jive Label Group CEO Barry Weiss left the company in March 2011 to become the new CEO of Island Def Jam and Universal Republic, which were both part of Universal Music Group. Weiss had been the RCA/Jive Label Group CEO since 2008 and was head of Jive Records since 1991.
q1: Barry Weiss became CEO of RCA/Jive in 2008. (true/false)
q2: Weiss headed Jive Records starting in 1991. (true/false)

story: The merger made Columbia and Epic sister labels to RCA Records, which was once owned by RCA which also owned CBS rival NBC. It also started the process of bringing BMG's Arista Records back under common ownership with its former parent Columbia Pictures, a Sony division since 1989, and also brought Arista founder Clive Davis back into the fold. Davis is still with Sony Music as Chief Creative Officer.
q1: Clive Davis works for Sony Music as Chief Creative Officer. (true/false)
q2: Columbia Pictures became a Sony division in 1995. (true/false)

story: In May 2012, Sony Music filed charges against the website IsoHunt. The plaintifs claims in the court document filed at the supreme court of British Columbia: "The IsoHunt Websites have been designed and are operated by the defendants with the sole purpose of profiting from rampant copyright infringement which defendants actively encourage, promote, authorize, induce, aid, abet, materially contribute to and commercially profit from."
q1: Sony Music sued IsoHunt in May 2012. (true/false)
q2: The case was filed in New York. (true/false)

story: Oklahoma City was settled on April 22, 1889, when the area known as the "Unassigned Lands" was opened for settlement in an event known as "The Land Run". Some 10,000 homesteaders settled the area that would become the capital of Oklahoma. The town grew quickly; the population doubled between 1890 and 1900. Early leaders of the development of the city included Anton Classen, John Shartel, Henry Overholser and James W. Maney.
q1: Oklahoma City was settled on April 22, 1889. (true/false)
q2: The settlement was called "The Land Walk". (true/false)

story: The "Core-to-Shore" project was created to relocate I-40 one mile (1.6 km) south and replace it with a boulevard to create a landscaped entrance to the city. This also allows the central portion of the city to expand south and connect with the shore of the Oklahoma River. Elements of "Core to Shore" were included in the MAPS 3 proposal approved by voters in late 2009.
q1: The Core-to-Shore project moved I-40 northward. (true/false)
q2: Elements of Core-to-Shore were in the MAPS 3 proposal. (true/false)
